item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is just enough mystery in this story to hold my attention until the end. For example, no one's ever been called out of the waiting room but the display starts at 23. Perhaps it's a psychological trick. It gives the person inside a false sense of hope. I really like that the last test subject was left in the waiting room so long that he doesn't remember what he was told to wait for.
One complaint I have is that it seems odd that the door is described as being near the cave entrance instead of inside the cave. Also it is hard for me to believe that the people inside loose track of time so easily when there is a clock on the wall, especially the woman who was only in there for six hours. That would have been easy to see on an analog clock. If the clock were broken so it didn't tell time but still kept ticking, that would make more sense.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Extra double thanks to you, Lils.

:>

Two-and-a-half years on, 100!

Thanks to all who've read it, and thanks to Roget especially.

Here's to 1000 ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why do we wait? All door are unlocked.

Masterful. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually the heartbreaking part for me. I DON'T like it, but there's such great writing to support the idea it's almost a shame to downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was great. I got quiet tingles. Soft, insidious effect, never trying to break into anything overstated or plot-twisty. "I don't know. The door was unlocked" fucked me up in all the right ways.

One thing I don't like - placing it inside a cave, and having the opening be a picture of a cave entrance. O, Death and Somewhere In the Between both did it, and I think a version of The Reliquary had it too for a while. The first two times I opened this page, I saw a cave entrance and checked out. I feel like this would be much better served with a picture of the room, or no picture at all (though I know Roget would hate the latter). Doesn't change my upvote, tho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn't anything to wait for. You just think there is, so you wait, forever and ever until someone tells you otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, but the complete lack of any indicator of the room's origin, purpose, or what exactly people wait for kills it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's almost not enough going on here, but the final log is really kind of unsettling. Like, something went terribly wrong here, and no one's sure why. I get the feeling no one ever will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"You can wait here in the sitting room, or sit here in the waiting room."
from the Firesign Theatre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was trying to imply that not every test was included with this documentation. I didn't make it more explicit because it's a minor detail and I feel like the Foundation wouldn't always put up roadsigns when they deliberately neglect to include something in classified documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, but

Tickets start at 024 and progress to 999 before running out.

And later it's mentioned that tickets 127 and higher were used in testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just… doesn't do much for me, to be perfectly honest. I didn't find myself moved by it, and the concept seemed a little too straightforward and basic for my liking - of course they aren't fully aware of how long they spent, of course they don't need to eat or sleep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's hard to write interesting tests about a person waiting in a room, we tried to have small moments of interest in each one to build off of stuff written earlier in the article and get in detail like the ticking clock. I hope it's short enough that you didn't find it interminably dull and I'm glad you enjoyed the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. Neat

Being stuck in a waiting room is a pretty ubiquitous experience, and you two take the that to a very serious extreme with there being nothing to wait for, yet people wait on. Its very Kafkaesque, and for that reason the article works for me.

I think you'd get a little more bang for your buck in the test log if you addressed what happens when multiple people are allowed to wait together, but that's bonus round for me.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a little of the generations of people living their whole lives in a waiting room in a palace (surviving on coffee and donuts) in Gene Wolfe's Book of the New Sun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't really do anything for me. There's a cool little bit of cruel irony at the end, but it failed to hold my interest prior to that point. The test logs were functional, but dull for the most part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this, I thought the mixture of ridiculous concept and scary execution was right up my street.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I will second the thanks to the folks who gave feedback, and further endorse Lily is a super cool collab partner. Hope you guys enjoy the wait!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hurrah! Finally another skip!

Massive thanks to my co-creator Roget, and a thank you to ChaoSera, Vezaz, Tuomey, LordStonefish, minmin, and Lex1nat0r for critting this in chat.

Image sourced from here, under CC-BY-SA 3.0 by Larry D. Moore.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Entrance_crystal_cave.jpg
